claim: Compressed morbidity from GLP-1 agonists and longevity science could reduce rather than expand late-life healthcare costs.
needs: Long-duration clinical outcome data at population level; CBO/CMS actuarial analysis showing net cost reduction after accounting for drug costs; evidence that morbidity savings exceed medication costs at scale

claim: The Minsky financial instability cycle is demographically conditioned, with demographic booms accelerating the hedge→speculative→Ponzi transition.
needs: Empirical methodology to isolate demographic effects from financial deregulation, policy, and other cyclical factors; cross-country panel studies testing the demographic-Minsky hypothesis

claim: Declining birth rates in developed markets will structurally compress the RMBS securitization pipeline, redirecting capital toward private credit and esoteric ABS.
needs: Decomposition of mortgage origination volume into refinancing vs. purchase components; evidence that demographic household formation decline dominates rate/refi cycles and GSE reform effects; longer time series data

claim: The 5y5y forward real rate encodes a demographic signal about long-run neutral rates.
needs: Larger cross-country sample beyond three data points; methodology to disentangle demographic signal from monetary regime, fiscal stance, and reserve currency confounders

claim: Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia fertility rates may decline faster than UN median projections, collapsing the 'next billion consumers' thesis sooner than expected.
needs: Evidence that smartphone-penetration fertility mechanism operates in Sub-Saharan African cultural contexts as it did in South Asia; updated fertility survey data from key SSA countries; assessment of whether income-based vs. information-based fertility decline models better explain recent trends

claim: Bank profitability as a second-round demographic effect — structurally declining loan origination in aging societies undermines bank business models.
needs: Empirical decomposition of European bank profitability decline into demographic vs. regulatory vs. competitive factors; cross-country panel data linking loan origination growth rates to working-age population growth; theoretical framework development
